title: A Quick Announcement
excerpt: Brighton's idea, dad is the tool to complete it.
body: A Quick Announcement...
A little update on something we've been working on behind the scenes.
And when I say "we," I mostly mean Brighton.
The truth is, DinoBoy Sticker Lab was never my idea.
It was his.
Like most things around here, I just got assigned the job of figuring out how to build it.
The idea started with Brighton's dinosaur sticker.
One kid. One drawing. One message.
A way to express what he was feeling in the middle of something no kid should have to experience.
What started as a sticker slowly became a bigger idea.
What if every kid fighting cancer had a way to create their own battle sticker?
Not something designed for them. Something designed by them.
Their words. Their humor. Their dinosaurs. Their monsters. Their battle cries. Their fight.
Kids spend so much time in hospitals having things happen to them. Appointments. Procedures. Scans. Treatments. Schedules. Adults making decisions.
Brighton wanted to create something that gave kids ownership.
A way to say: "This is who I am." "This is what I'm fighting." "This is my sticker."
So over the last few months we've been building DinoBoy Sticker Lab.
And by "we," I mean Brighton comes up with ideas and I become unpaid labor.
Honestly, that's been our relationship for years.
The website is starting to come together. His original sticker is there. The story is there. The vision is starting to take shape.
The goal isn't really stickers.
The goal is giving kids a voice.
Giving them something that belongs to them in the middle of a world where so much feels outside their control.
Like so many things Brighton touches, it started with him wanting to help someone else.
That's one of the things I admire most about him.
Even now. Even while dealing with pain. Even while fighting his own battle.
He's still thinking about other kids.
I'm just one of the tools in his toolbox helping him build it.
And honestly, that's a pretty good job to have.
DinoBoy Sticker Lab is coming.
A little loud. A little weird. A little chaotic. Entirely Brighton.
We are hoping to launch the site within the next month. We promise you will be the first to know!

title: Faith in Kindness
excerpt: Getting help from family and friends
body: I’ve been thinking a lot about faith lately.
Not because I’ve suddenly found it.
Because people keep bringing it to my front door.
I grew up Mormon.
I served a mission in Taiwan. I worked hard. I followed the rules. I believed deeply. I came home, married Paula in the San Diego Temple, started a family, and tried to build a good life.
Years later, my questions became bigger than my certainty.
Some of the answers I was given didn’t make sense anymore.
So I stepped away.
That journey deserves its own story someday.
This one is about suffering.
One thing I’ve learned through Brighton’s illness is that people desperately want pain to make sense.
They want an explanation.
A formula.
A reason.
Because the alternative is terrifying.
The alternative is admitting that sometimes terrible things happen and nobody gets to tie it up neatly with a bow.
Over the years we’ve heard everything.
“Everything happens for a reason.”
“God doesn’t make mistakes.”
“If you have enough faith.”
“God has a plan.”
And I know most of those comments come from a place of love.
But sometimes love is wrong.
Sometimes people say things because they make themselves feel better, not because they help the family living through it.
The one that gets me the most is:
“God doesn’t make mistakes.”
Really?
Go spend a day on the oncology floor at CHOC.
Or Rady Children’s.
Watch children missing limbs.
Watch children recovering from surgeries.
Watch children who can’t walk.
Watch parents sleeping in chairs.
Watch nurses become family because mom and dad have to work.
Watch a fourteen-year-old boy struggle through pain that most adults couldn’t tolerate.
Then tell me again that none of this is a mistake.
Because from where I’m sitting, if this is all part of some perfect plan, then I have some questions.
The other one that makes my blood boil is:
“If you had more faith…”
Let’s stop right there.
What exactly are you saying?
That families whose children survive believed harder?
That families whose children die didn’t?
That somehow the outcome is tied to the quality of someone’s belief?
That is an unbelievably cruel thing to place on a parent, whether you mean it that way or not.
The reality is we’ve looked at everything.
We’ve talked to experts.
We’ve chased treatments.
We’ve researched.
We’ve asked questions.
We’ve fought.
We don’t need medical advice from TikTok.
We don’t need miracle cures forwarded from Facebook.
And we definitely don’t need somebody explaining our own experience back to us.
What we need is much simpler.
Bring food.
Mow the lawn.
Walk the dog.
Take the trash out.
Sit with us.
Tell a joke.
Give a hug.
Or just show up and say nothing at all.
The people who have helped us the most were never the people with answers.
They were the people willing to carry a little bit of the weight.
After four years of this, I’ve become deeply suspicious of certainty.
But I have tremendous faith in kindness.
I’ve seen what that can do.

title: A Visit From Our Hero
imageAlt: Dr. Misaghi pays Brighton a visit
excerpt: Brighton's Pediatric Orthopedic Oncology Surgeon pays a visit
body: This is Brighton’s Pediatric Orthopedic Oncology Surgeon, Dr. Amir Misaghi.

Mayo trained. One of the most specialized surgeons you can find. The type of person who has gone through more schooling and training than most of us can even comprehend. The type of surgeon families pray they can find when life goes sideways.
And somehow, beyond all of that, he is one of the kindest people we have ever met.

Brighton had a routine follow-up appointment scheduled with him tomorrow, but because of pain we had to cancel it. Dr. Misaghi had his nurse call to check in and ask why. Paula explained what was going on, and his response was simple:
“Can I come visit him?”
Not schedule another appointment.
Not squeeze us in next week.
Come visit him.
His nurse, who has been with us through so much of this journey, asked if she could come too.
Tonight, Dr. Misaghi came to our house.
And honestly, it was one of the most touching moments we’ve had through all of this.
He stayed for an hour and a half and barely even examined Brighton. He mostly just sat with him and talked. About life. About surgery day. About random things. About how proud he is of him.
There is something incredibly powerful about someone with that level of intelligence and accomplishment still understanding how important simple human connection is.
This is a man who could easily separate himself emotionally. He could be clinical. Distant. Untouchable.
Instead, he hugs us multiple times. Tells us he loves Brighton. Tells us he will come over anytime we need him.
Who does that?
At one point, Brighton looked at him as he was leaving and said:
“Dr. Misaghi, you are my hero.”
And honestly, I echoed it immediately.
He is all of our hero.
The thing I continue to notice through this journey is that Brighton pulls people toward him. Everywhere he goes, people want to help him, protect him, talk to him, love him, and be around him.
He changes people.
And so does Dr. Misaghi.
We need more people like both of them in this world.

title: Chemo Round Complete
excerpt: Day getting an infusion and keeping things afloat
body: Hi everyone, I’m Alex’s sister, Hayley, and he handed me the keyboard for this update. I recently returned home after spending two weeks with the family, and I wanted to share a little of what I saw and felt while I was there.

It’s hard to fully grasp what a family goes through with a cancer diagnosis. Imagine sand, thick and heavy, rising around you until you can barely move. Four years ago, when they first got the diagnosis, it was a foot of sand. A horrible thing to hear, but there was still hope and space to breathe. Slowly, year by year, the sand kept rising. Up to their waists, then their chests. Alex, Paula, Brighton, and Izzie all learning to keep moving through life with that weight pressing in around them.

When I arrived, the sand felt like it had filled the entire house. Blinding. Suffocating. Heavy enough to make my eyes water the moment I walked through the door. And yet, I was amazed at how well they had learned to keep moving through something that nearly knocked me over immediately.

The first things I noticed were the piles of Legos covering the dining room table, entire armies built with unopened boxes stacked nearby, a neat pile of hospital bills on the kitchen counter, and a large bed sitting in the middle of the living room with baskets of supplies underneath and a wheelchair beside it.
Such strange things to see in the center of a home.
But to them, it is normal now.
Working a full-time job while running your own company is hard enough, then add your sick, sleeping son in the middle of the house.
Going to high school, taking AP classes, and recovering from a soccer injury is hard enough, then add your brother unable to move himself a few feet away in the living room.
Running a business, making dinner, cleaning the house, and managing a family is hard enough, then add being a nurse at home for your son who is in pain.
The bills still come in. The groceries still need buying. The dishes still need washing.
Life keeps moving whether you’re ready or not.

I quickly found my own small routine while I was there. Filling juice cups, walking the dogs, organizing cupboards while everyone buzzed around me. I moved Brighton’s feet every hour or so, turned on calming music, watched movies with him.

And then, sweet Brighton, who doesn’t want to cause too much trouble, loves chatting about philosophy and World War II, advocates for himself so bravely in hospital rooms, and randomly breaks into singing “Man or Muppet” from The Muppets. One of the movies we watched together that he loved was The Devil Wears Prada.

When he needed to be moved and the fear crept in, we practiced deep expanding belly breaths together, filling up his diaphragm like a balloon until the panic settled. After a few days, Brighton trusted me to help move his fragile body.
But mostly, he slept.
The days were long and quiet and heavy.
The nights were my favorite.
That’s when Brighton was awake and talking. When everyone gathered together on the couch. We watched movies, played trivia, laughed, and talked about everything and nothing all at once.
Somewhere in those two weeks, without even realizing it, I learned how to move through the sand too.
There is nothing correct to say to someone carrying the weight this family carries. No amount of help that fully lifts that burden.
But I am so grateful I got to be there.
And even more grateful they let me in.
Pictures: These are pictures Hayley took while we weren't looking, for the most part.
